markdownRemark.excerpt: This post describes why and how we implemented a garbage collector in our Xbox application on top of Redux and in addition to the JS engine’s regular garbage…
markdownRemark.html:
This post describes why and how we implemented a garbage collector in our Xbox application on top of Redux and in addition to the JS engine’s regular garbage collector.

The Xbox application is a regular React + Redux application that runs in a native web view wrapper on Microsoft’s TODO: find name of M$ frameweork web app technology. In order to allow users to play music in the background, we are also shipping a native C++ playback layer with the app. A thin API-wrapper allows for communication between the web layer and the native layer.

Background audio was not the only reason for us to ship a native player. We also had to make sure that our application was not using TODO: find exact number here more that XMB when put into background mode. Applications with more than XMB TODO: exact number can and will get killed by the Xbox application scheduler when it notices that the system or a game needs more memory.

The native player we are using is the same one we are using in our mobile applications which means it’s otimized for low memory consumption. Our initial tests showed a low-enough memory usage of the application and were content that we would not hit the maximum memory limit anytime soon.

The memory leak that was just a cache

At a later stage we ran more memory tests and we noticed that our memory consumption was steadily growing with time (for sessions with usage time of more than one hour) and never shrinking and so our hunt for a memory leak began. We quickly identified our Redux store as the part of our application that just kept growing which makes complete sense since Redux does not define explicit ways to remove parts of the state in order to save memory.

Just to be clear, I’m not blaming Redux here for clogging up our user’s memory! Memory consumption should not be your concern when you are building a web application unless you have to work in an environment that is enforcing strict memory limits like the Xbox application schedulero or when you are building an application that users are spending a lot of time in without reloading the page or not closing the app (e.g. in case of Electron apps).

Normalized state

Our Redux store is normalized, which means that we organize incoming data into it’s different type entities instead of keeping nested structures. Working with nested structures can be very tricky e.g. when you want to propagate changes of an entity. Let’s look at an example:

{
  playlists: [
    {
      id: 'p1',
      tracks: [{ id: 't1' }, { id: 't2' }],
    },
    {
      id: 'p2',
      tracks: [{ id: 't1' }, { id: 't3' }],
    },
  ]
}

The above code represents a user’s playlists. They have two playlists (p1 and p2) that each have two tracks. p1 contains tracks t1 and t2, p2 contains tracks t1 and t3. If the user now wants to update the title of track t1, we have to iterate over all playlists and all tracks in those playlists because track t1 might be in other playlists as well. This problem becomes even more complicated if you have other collections of tracks in your redux store (e.g. a user’s liked tracks), because then you will have to essentially iterate over all track collections in your store only to make sure you’re updating the track in all places.

{
  entities: {
    playlists: {
      p1: {},
      p2: {}
    },
    tracks: {
      t1: {},
      t2: {},
      t3: {}
    }
  }
}

A normalized state like the one above allows for better change propagation because it a) removes duplicates, so you only ever have to change a single object and it b) merges entities from different requests into a single “entities” store so that you don’t have to worry about updating tracks from other collections. In this case, we would only have to update the title of the object at entities.tracks.t1 and it would propagate correctly throught our entire application.

Another great property of normalized state is that it becomes trivial to check if you already have a locally cached instance of e.g. a track that you want to render. This can decrease the amount of requests your application makes by a lot.

{
  entities: {
    playlists: {
      p1: {},
      p2: {}
    },
    tracks: {
      ...
    }
  },
  playlists: ['p1', 'p2']
}

In the example above you can see that our playlist representation now only consists of an array of ids that “point” to playlist entities.

If you are interested in learning more about normalization and how to add it to your application, I recommend checking out normalizr.

Garbage collection in JavaScript

With every request we made, we were increasing the amount of objects in our entities store and we never removed objects, even if they were not needed anymore. Usually in JavaScript, your data gets garbage collected once you remove all references to it (e.g. myState = null) which, in case all your data is encapsulated in your React components, happens automatically when React unmounts your component. When you use Redux though, the Redux store will always have a reference to all your data so it will never get released.

Another way to free memory in JavaScript is to just reload the page every now and then when you detect that the session is very long already or when a lot of objects have accumulated in your entities store. Of course this requires that your app is able to reloading quickly so that users don’t get frustrated with your site’s performance and you should make sure that your users are not playing any media because you really don’t want to interrupt them in that case.

Since in our use case we want to save memory for a background-listening experience, we were not able to just reload the page. We came to the conclusion that we had to build a garbage collector on top of our Redux store to free memory efficiently. This is not the first time we’re building a GC algorithm into one of our applications, though. The main website at soundcloud.com had a GC system built into it’s model layer from the very beginning. Since the main site is built with Backbone we could not reuse the GC implementation for the Xbox application.

Tracing garbage collection

When we looked at the different approaches to garbage collect, we saw a striking similarity between how tracing garbage collection works and the way Redux works in general.

A tracing garbage collector is working in two steps (mark & sweep):

  1. Mark: It marks objects that still have references.
  2. Sweep: It scans all objects in memory and removes all the ones that are not marked.

If we would implement a garbage collector in Redux this way, we would never be able to remove objects because there will always be references to all objects. In order to make this concept work we need to be able to remove references and therefore find a way to determine which parts of the state are not needed anymore. If we can identify unused state, we can remove the state and all it’s referenced entities.

Redux provides, by design, all the tools necessary to identify used and unused state. Each component’s selector function (mapStateToProps) tells you which part of the state it needs so we can use those to keep track of state that is not used anymore. The only problem is that we don’t have a list of all selectors that are currently on screen but that is exactly where our garbage collector library comes in.

Our Redux garbage collector

The first thing that our library does is to keep track of selector functions that are currently on screen. We do this by wrapping our component with a higher-order component that mimicks react-redux’s connect API.

function (mapStateToProps) {
  return compose(
    connect(mapStateToProps),
    lifecycle({
      componentDidMount() {
        // register on-screen selector
        registerSelector(mapStateToProps, this.props, this)
      },
      componentWillUnmount() {
        // remove on-screen selector
        removeSelector(this)
      },
    })
  );
}

(I left out the other parameters of connect here to shorten the example code)

We are using recompose to create a composed component that a) connects to the Redux store and b) registers and removes the selector when the component mounts / unmounts.

let selectors = []
function registerSelector(selectorFn, props, instance) {
  selectors.push({ selectorFn, props, instance })
}

In registerSelector we are adding the selector to our list of on-screen selectors alongside with a reference of the passed props and a reference to the component’s instance.

function removeSelector(instance) {
  selectors = selectors.filter(selector => selector.instance !== instance)
}

We need the reference to the instance of the component because we use it to remove a selector from the list. It is important to mention here that we need to check agains the reference of the component here and not agains a reference of the selector itself because we want to prevent that we remove a selector of the component that is going to unmount, independent of the selector function. Another component, one that is still on-screen, might use the same selector function but we want to keep that one.

At this point we are so far as keeping track of on-screen selectors, next we have to implement the actual part of the library that removes unused state.

As described above, the bulk of our data is now organised in the entities part of our Redux store which means that one is the part of the store that we should remove unused objects from. All other parts of the store are only id-references or arrays of id-references and they are not consuming that much data so we can keep those.

One problem that we’re facing now though, is that selector functions can return arbitrary shapes of data and they might aggregate data in a way that it does not represent the pure data of the Redux store. One example would be a component that renders the title of a track.

function (store, { trackId }) {
  return {
    title: store.entities.tracks[userId].title
  };
}

The result of that function will be something like:

{
  title: 'Redbone'
}

Just looking at this result, the garbage collector will not be able to determine which parts of the state are used to compute the title. In order to make sense of a selector’s result we need it to somehow return it’s dependencies in addition to its regular result. This is why all garbage-collectable selectors have to return data in the following shape:

{
  result: {
    title: 'Redbone'
  },
  dependencies: [{
    entity: 'track',
    id: 't1'
  }]
}

The data returned in dependencies gives us clear instructions on which type of entity and which instance of this entity this selector depends on. Now, returning this extra metadata from all of your components is pretty tedious, which is why we wrapped mainly use these selectors in specialized container components that abstract this extra overhead away for us:

<WithTrack trackId="t1">
  (track) => <TrackComponent track={track}>
</WithTrack>

In this example, WithTrack is connected to the Redux store and it makes sure to return the proper metadata. This removes a lot of the extra overhead created by the garbage collection library.

Taking out the garbage

The actual garbage collection is intiated by a regular Redux action, let’s call it the sweep action since we are in the sweep phase of the original algorithm now.

function reducer(state, action) {
  if (action.type === SWEEP_ACTION_TYPE) {
    return sweep(state, action.selectors)
  } else {
    return combinedReducers(state, action)
  }
}

The sweep action is “intercepted” in our top level reducer which is passing the action to our sweep reducer.

function sweep(oldState, selectors) {
  const entities = selectors
    .reduce(
      (dependencies, { selector, props }) =>
        dependencies.concat(selector(props).depenedencies),
      []
    )
    .reduce(
      (newState, { entity, id }) =>
        (newState.entities[entity][id] = oldState.entities[entity][id]),
      {}
    )

  return {
    ...oldState,
    entities,
  }
}

There are essentially three things happening in our sweep reducer.

  1. We create a list of all dependencies by iterating over and evaluating all selectors. Here we make use of the stored props as well because we need to pass them to each selector function in order to get the correct dependencies. At the end we end up with an array that contains the dependencies of all components that are on-screen right now.
  2. We start off with an empty object and then copy over each required entity from the oldState to the newState by iterating over the dependencies. The result of this is a fresh entities object that only consists of entities that are required by mounted components. This essentially performs the sweep that we know from the algorithm but instead of removing objects from the state, we actually started with a clean state and then added objects to it. Instead of mark & sweep we basically execute a mark & keep.
  3. We return the garbage-collected state with the slimmed-down entities object. Our store now only contains the objects that are necessary to render the current screen.

It depends on the nature of your application, when it’s best to start the garbage collection. Some applications might just do it at a certain interval, some applications might want to check if they are using a lot of memory and others might just execute is every time the user moved to a different major screen. There is no correct way to do this for all different kinds of applications.

Performance

We have not obeserved a slow-down of our application due to our garbage-collection process. Your components are also not rerendered, because the reference to the part of the state they depend on stays the same. In general, if you keep your selectors pure, you should not see a performance hit.

You might think to yourself now: “Wow, this would really make my application so much more efficient, I will add it right away!!!“. Please, before you consider adding a garbage collection process to your application, check if you actually have problems with too-high memory usage. I would argue that 99% of Redux applications don’t require a custom GC process as they’re either too short-lived or they don’t have strict memory restrictions.

This system is working very well for us and we will keep using it on new applications when needed. We would love to know what you think about our approach if you have dealt with similar problems :)

Todo

  • What is the allowed background memory usage on Xbox?
  • Create Github repo with reference implementation?

    connect(mapStateToProps),
    lifecycle({
      componentDidMount() {

        registerSelector(mapStateToProps, this.props, this)

        removeSelector(this)

(I left out the other parameters of connect here to shorten the example code)

  selectors.push({ selectorFn, props, instance })

  result: {
    title: 'Redbone'

    id: 't1'

Mark & Keep in our case!

frontmatter.title: Garbage collection in long-running JavaScript applications
